originalTitle: UN votes to recognise enslavement of Africans as 'gravest crime against humanity'
title: A Big 'Sorry' for a Very Sad Past!
content:
Hello, My Amazing Friends!

Hey everyone! It's your friend, Reporter Ama, here, bringing you super important news from our beautiful continent of Africa! I hope you're all having a wonderful day filled with sunshine and smiles. Today, we're talking about something really big that happened far away, but it's super important for us and for everyone in the world. Guess what? The grown-ups at a very special club, a club for almost all the countries in the whole world, made a really big decision. This club is called the United Nations, or UN for short. They had a big meeting and talked about something very, very old and very, very sad.

A Big, Important Meeting!

The United Nations is like a super-duper big school council, but for the whole planet! Countries from all over the world send their leaders to talk and make rules together. They try to make the world a safer, fairer, and happier place for everyone. Recently, they all got together to talk about history. Not just any history, but a very difficult part of history that hurt many, many people, especially people from Africa. It's important to learn from history so we don't make the same mistakes again.

What Was the Big News?

So, what did they decide? The UN said that something called 'enslavement' of Africans was the 'gravest crime against humanity.' Wow, that sounds like a lot of big words, doesn't it? Let me explain what they mean in simple words. 'Gravest crime against humanity' means it was the very, very worst thing you could ever do to a lot of people. It was a super bad act that caused immense pain and suffering to countless families and communities, and it should never, ever happen again.

What Does 'Enslavement' Mean?

'Enslavement' is a very sad word. It means that a long, long time ago, some people were taken from their homes in Africa. They were forced to leave their families and their land. They were then made to work for free, without any pay, and were treated very, very badly. Imagine not being able to choose what you eat, where you live, or even who you can be with. Imagine being told what to do all day, every day, and not being allowed to be free. That's what enslavement was like. It stole people's freedom, their joy, and their lives. Did you know? Enslavement went on for hundreds of years! Many people from different parts of Africa were taken across the big ocean to other countries. This period of history brought so much sorrow to our continent.

Why Is This Decision So Important Now?

Even though this happened a very, very long time ago, the sadness and the unfairness of it still affect people today. The UN's decision is like saying, "Yes, this really happened, and it was terribly, terribly wrong." It’s important for everyone to understand this part of history. It helps us remember to always be kind, to treat everyone fairly, and to stand up for people's freedom. This decision is a big step towards healing old wounds. Did you know? Learning about history helps us understand the world better. It teaches us about courage, resilience, and the importance of justice for everyone.

Saying 'Sorry' – An Apology

One of the big things the UN asked for is an 'apology.' What's an apology? It's when you say 'I'm sorry' after you've done something wrong. When you apologize, it shows that you understand you hurt someone, and you wish you hadn't. For something as big and as hurtful as enslavement, a very big apology is needed. It's a way for countries and people who were involved in that sad time to say, "We are truly sorry for the pain that was caused to African people." Saying sorry won't change the past, but it can help us move forward. It helps people feel seen and respected. It's a sign that we want to build a better future together, where everyone is treated with dignity.

Helping to Make Things Better – A Special Fund

The UN also talked about something called a 'reparations fund.' A 'fund' is like a big piggy bank. This special piggy bank would collect money to help make things better for the communities and descendants of those who suffered from enslavement. Think about it like this: if someone accidentally breaks your toy, they might say sorry and also help you fix it, or get you a new one. This fund is a bit like that, but on a much bigger scale. It's about helping to repair the damage that was done. This money could be used for many good things. It could help build new schools, so more children can learn. It could help build hospitals, so more people can stay healthy. It could help create opportunities for people to start businesses and grow. Did you know? When we work together and help each other, we can make big, positive changes in the world. Sharing and helping is a super-power!

Who Are the United Nations?

Let's talk a little more about this amazing UN club! The United Nations was started after a very big war, many, many years ago. Leaders from around the world wanted a place where they could talk instead of fight. Their main goals are to keep peace, help countries work together to solve problems, protect human rights (which means making sure everyone is treated fairly and has freedom), and help people in need. It's like a big team working for the good of the world! This decision about enslavement is a great example of the UN trying to make the world more fair and just for everyone, by recognizing the wrongs of the past.

Why This Decision Matters for Everyone

This decision from the UN is a big step for our continent, Africa. It means that the world is listening and acknowledging the incredible pain and injustice that our ancestors faced. It's a moment of truth and recognition. It's also important for all children, everywhere, to learn about this. It teaches us about being fair, about respecting everyone, no matter where they come from or what their skin colour is. We must always remember that all people are equal and deserve to be free. Did you know? Every person in the world has rights, like the right to be safe, the right to learn, and the right to be treated with respect. The UN helps to protect these rights for everyone.

Learning from History, Building a Better Future!

So, what do we learn from all this, my dear friends? We learn that even very old wrongs can be recognized and talked about. We learn that saying 'sorry' is important. And we learn that working together to help others is super powerful. Let's all promise to be kind to each other every day. Let's stand up for what is fair and right. Let's make sure that everyone we meet feels respected and loved. By doing this, we are building a better, brighter future for Africa and for the whole world! That's all for today, my amazing little news explorers! Stay curious, stay kind, and remember to always shine your brightest light! This is Reporter Ama, signing off from Africa. Bye for now!

originalTitle: South Africa's police chief to appear in court over controversial health contract
title: Big News in South Africa: A Special Police Boss Goes to Court!
content:
Hello, super kids of Africa! It's your friendly news reporter, here to share some important news from our lovely continent. Today, we're zooming into a country called South Africa! It's a land with big animals like lions and elephants, and lots of wonderful people. We have a story about a very important person there.

Who is the Police Chief?

Imagine your school. You have a headteacher, right? Someone who is the boss of the school. Well, in a country, the police have a boss too! This special person is called the Police Chief. They are like the super-duper leader of all the police officers in the whole country. What do police officers do? They are our heroes! They help keep everyone safe. They make sure people follow the rules. They help if someone is lost or if there's trouble. They wear cool uniforms and sometimes drive cars with flashing lights! The Police Chief makes sure all these brave officers do their very best job. **Did you know?** South Africa has many different kinds of police officers, some even help protect special animals like rhinos and elephants!

A Special Promise Called a Contract

Now, let's talk about something called a "contract." It's a big word, but it just means a special promise or an agreement. Imagine you and your friend make a promise to play together after school. That's a bit like a contract! It's an understanding of what will happen. Governments, like the people who run a country, make lots of contracts. They make promises with companies to build roads, or to get food for schools, or to buy important things for hospitals. These contracts are very, very important because they help the country work well and keep everyone happy and safe.

What is a Health Contract?

Our story today is about a "health contract." What does "health" mean? It means keeping our bodies strong and well. It's about doctors, nurses, hospitals, and medicines. So, a "health contract" is a special promise or agreement about things that help people stay healthy. Maybe it was a contract to buy special machines for hospitals. Or maybe it was to get important medicines for people who are sick. These things are very, very important because everyone needs to be healthy to learn, play, and grow!

Why is This Contract Special?

This health contract became a bit "controversial." That's another big word! "Controversial" means that some people are not happy about it. They think something might not have been done fairly or correctly. It's like when you and your friend disagree about who goes first in a game. People disagree about how this health contract was handled. Some people might think the money for the health things was too much. Or maybe they think the things bought weren't good enough. Or perhaps the promise wasn't kept properly. When people are worried like this, it's important to find out what happened and make things right. **Did you know?** Good health helps you run faster, jump higher, and learn better in school! So health contracts are super important for everyone.

Going to a Special Place: The Court

When there are big questions like this, and people want to find out the truth, they go to a special building called a "court." A court is a place where rules are very important. It's like a very serious classroom where grown-ups try to solve problems and find out what is fair. In court, there are special people called judges. Judges are like very wise teachers. Their job is to listen carefully to everyone and decide what is true and fair. There are also lawyers who help people tell their side of the story and ask questions.

What Happens When Someone Goes to Court?

When the Police Chief goes to court, it means that someone wants to ask questions about the health contract. He will "stand in the dock." The dock is a special place in the courtroom where the person who is being asked questions stands. It's a chance for everyone to understand what happened. The "charges" are what the Police Chief is being asked about. It means someone thinks that some rules might have been broken when the health contract was made. But remember, just because someone goes to court, it doesn't mean they are guilty right away. It means the court will listen and find out the truth, which is very fair. The people in charge, the "authorities," haven't told us *exactly* what those charges are yet. They want to make sure everything is ready for court. It's like waiting for all the clues to be gathered before telling the whole story so everyone can understand. **Did you know?** In court, everyone gets a chance to speak and explain themselves. It's a very fair process to find out what happened!

Why is This Important for All of Us?

You might wonder why this is important for you, a wonderful kid in Africa. Well, it's important because it shows that everyone, no matter how important they are, must follow the rules. Just like you follow rules at home and at school, grown-ups in big jobs must follow big rules too. When rules are followed, everyone benefits. If money for health contracts is used properly, then hospitals get good equipment, and people get the medicine they need. This helps everyone in South Africa and makes the country stronger and healthier. This court hearing is about making sure things are done correctly and fairly for all the people in South Africa. It's about finding answers and making sure justice is served. **Did you know?** South Africa has 11 official languages! That's a lot of ways to say "hello" and share stories!

What Happens Next?

The Police Chief will go to court, and the judge will listen carefully to all the stories. We will learn more about the "charges" and what exactly happened with the health contract. It might take some time, because finding the truth needs careful listening and thinking. We will keep watching and tell you more when we know! For now, remember to always follow the rules, be fair to your friends, and stay healthy! That's all from your kid-friendly news reporter today. Stay tuned for more amazing stories from our incredible continent!
summary: The top police boss in South Africa will go to a special court to answer questions about a promise (contract) made for health things, because some people are worried about it.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows that everyone, even very important people, must follow the rules. It helps make sure that money used for health in South Africa is spent correctly so everyone can stay healthy and strong.

originalTitle: Senegal appeal to be heard 'as swiftly as possible'
title: Senegal appeal to be heard 'as swiftly as possible'
content: The Court of Arbitration for Sport (Cas) says it will rule on Senegal's appeal against being stripped of the Africa Cup of Nations title "as swiftly as possible".

originalTitle: Northern Ireland deserve Italy's respect - Buffon
title: Northern Ireland deserve Italy's respect - Buffon
content: World Cup winner Gianluigi Buffon says Italy have been fully concentrated on overcoming Northern Ireland as the sides prepare to face each other in Bergamo on Thursday.
